heading: MODULUS DECIMUSNONUS
card_summary: This section opens the Nineteenth Module (Modulus Decimusnonus) of the Eighth Tone of the Third Canticle, under the heading "Omne bonum" (Every Good / All That Is Good). Zorzi argues that God alone is the Supreme Good in an absolute and proper sense, drawing on Hermes Trismegistus (from the Corpus Hermeticum), Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite, Proclus (via Theodorus), and the New Testament. Key scriptural citations include Christ's rebuke "Nemo bonus nisi solus Deus" (Luke 18:19), the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:9), Psalm 144/145 ("Aperiente te manum tuam"), and Paul's 1 Corinthians 4:7 ("Quid habes quod non accepisti"). Zorzi harmonizes Hermetic and Neoplatonic doctrines of the Good-as-source with Christian theology of participation: creatures receive goodness and beauty only by participation in God, who is both the summum bonum and the summum pulchrum. The section closes with Christ as Mediator restoring all things to union with the Archetype of the Good.
essay:
## MODULUS DECIMUSNONUS — "Omne bonum" (Every Good / All That Is Good)

### The Latin Title and Its Meaning

The heading *Modulus Decimusnonus* designates the Nineteenth Module within the Eighth Tone (*Tonus Octavus*) of Zorzi's vast Third Canticle (*Canticum Tertium*). The Latin incipit *Omne bonum* — "Every Good" or "All That Is Good" — announces the governing theme: an inquiry into the nature of the Supreme Good and its communication to creatures. The Italian heading confirms this: *Ogni bene*, with the first subsection titled "Cos'è il bene che è detto sommo e vero" — "What is the Good that is called the highest and true Good."

### Content and Argument

Rather than functioning as a bare title page, this module is a fully developed speculative essay that aligns Zorzi's characteristic three-strand cord: Hermeticism, Neoplatonism, and Christian Scripture. The opening methodological remark is itself revealing — Zorzi insists that because God is *simplicissimum* (utterly simple), no discursive argument or sensory evidence can reach him; only divine illumination can guide the reader.

From Hermes Trismegistus (likely *Corpus Hermeticum* II and X) Zorzi draws the foundational axiom: God *is* the Good itself, not a being that *has* goodness. This is harmonized with Christ's saying "Nemo bonus nisi solus Deus" (Luke 18:19) and the Pauline insistence that all received gifts derive wholly from God (1 Cor 4:7). The distinction between absolute goodness (proper to God alone) and participated goodness (possible for creatures, and claimed by Christ in calling himself the Good Shepherd) is carefully maintained.

Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite supplies the cosmological framework: as the sun illuminates by its very being, God as *substantiale bonum* radiates goodness and beauty proportionally to all existents. The identification of the *summum bonum* with the *summum pulchrum* — a Platonic inheritance mediated through Dionysius and the Florentine Academicians — leads to the Proclean teaching (via "magnus Theodorus") that all things pray to the First Principle except the First Principle itself.

The module concludes with a soteriological arc: Christ as Mediator and Advocate (1 John 2:1; Hebrews 9:24) draws all things back to union with the Archetypal Good, restoring the primordial harmony that is the animating metaphor of the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*.
